name: PARO therapeutic robot
short_description: A robotic baby harp seal with tactile, light, and sound sensors used for animal-assisted-style therapy in hospitals and elder care.
long_description: PARO is a plush, seal-shaped social robot designed by Takanori Shibata at Japan's AIST and sold commercially since 2004. Covered in fur over twelve tactile sensors, with whiskers, light and posture sensing, and three microphones, it responds to petting and voice, learns its name, and adapts its behavior to elicit calm and emotional engagement. It matters for everyday physical AI because it shows how an affective, animal-like body can deliver measurable care benefits for people with dementia and autism, and it became one of the first social robots cleared as a U.S. Class 2 medical device.

images.caption: PARO therapeutic robot seal on display at Wired NextFest, Navy Pier.
images.alt_text: A small white robotic baby harp seal with dark eyes and soft fur, resembling a plush toy.

interactions.title: Pet it and it answers back
interactions.description: Stroke the fur and PARO moves its tail, opens and closes its eyes, turns toward your hand, and coos with cries recorded from real harp seals; pat it somewhere it dislikes and it wriggles away.

interactions.title: Give it a name and it learns to come when called
interactions.description: Residents pick a name for the seal and, over repeated use, PARO learns to respond to it along with greetings and praise, gradually favoring the actions that earn the most affection.

interactions.title: Awake by day, sleepy at night
interactions.description: Unlike a real baby seal, PARO is programmed to be lively during the day and drift off at night, so its rhythm matches the ward routine rather than fighting it.

interactions.title: Cradle on the ward calms agitation in 20 minutes
interactions.description: On a Penn Presbyterian acute-care unit, patients pet and talk to two seals named Lucy and Ricky; clinicians saw agitation drop within the first 20 minutes and pain scores fall by the end of a session, with the seal distracting people from pulling at their IV lines or wandering.

interactions.title: Switched on, the cuddle does more than a plush toy
interactions.description: In a controlled study, touching PARO while it was powered ON produced significantly greater pain and stress relief than touching the identical robot turned OFF — evidence that its responsive movement, not just its soft fur, is doing the work.
interactions.source_label: Geng et al. — touching PARO ON vs OFF

interactions.title: Talking to it 'like a child' at home
interactions.description: In family homes, people with dementia voluntarily petted and spoke to PARO as they would a small child; caregivers reported calmer behavior, more shared conversation, and less of the guilt and supervision burden of care.
interactions.source_label: PMC — PARO in family-based dementia care

interactions.title: A gentle lesson in handling
interactions.description: Because the whiskers and twelve tactile sensors register where and how hard it is touched, PARO could nudge a child or distressed adult toward gentler contact — leaning in and cooing for soft strokes, shrinking away from rough grabs — turning the seal into quiet feedback on touch.

sources.title: Paro (robot)
sources.access_notes: Overview of inventor, AIST origin, sensors, behavior, and FDA Class 2 device classification.
